question: What first drew you to Classics, and how has that interest developed since?
lookingFor: A genuine origin story with intellectual growth — not just childhood myths but a deepening engagement with the ancient world.

bullets: Identify a specific moment, text, or experience that sparked the interest
Show how the interest has evolved from surface-level fascination to deeper engagement
Connect that journey to why a university course is the logical next step
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It started with a battered copy of the Iliad I found on a family bookshelf when I was fourteen. I was hooked by the characters, but what kept me going was realising how much the poem was actually asking — about honour, grief, what we owe each other. I started reading around it: some Herodotus, a lot of secondary scholarship, and then I picked up Latin and found myself reading Virgil's response to Homer across eight centuries. That conversation between texts is what I want to spend the next few years inside properly, with the linguistic tools to read both in the original.

commonMistakes: Saying 'I've always loved Greek myths' with nothing more specific
Jumping to university ambitions without grounding the interest in real reading
Describing a passive attraction to ancient history rather than active engagement with texts
followUps: Which text or author has challenged you the most, and why?

question: Why study Classics at university rather than continuing on your own?
lookingFor: A clear sense of what structured study offers that independent reading cannot — language, depth, scholarly debate, method.

bullets: Identify a specific gap that independent reading leaves — usually language, critical method, or access to debates
Show awareness of what university Classics actually involves beyond general reading
Demonstrate that self-directed study is already happening, not just hoped for
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been reading widely, but I keep hitting walls I can't break through on my own. My Latin is decent but I can't yet read Tacitus at speed, and my Greek is still elementary — I need structured teaching to get there. Beyond language, I want to engage with proper historiographical debates: is Thucydides reliable? How do we read material culture alongside texts? Those arguments happen in seminars, not in isolation. Independent reading got me hungry; university is where I want to learn how to actually do Classics.

commonMistakes: Vague answers about 'deepening knowledge' with no specifics
Showing no evidence of existing independent study
Treating Classics as just history rather than a philological and interpretive discipline
followUps: Which aspect of the course — language, literature, history, or archaeology — do you expect to find most challenging?

question: Why do you want to study Classics here specifically, rather than at another university?
lookingFor: Specific, researched reasons tied to this department — modules, faculty, approach — not generic prestige.

bullets: Name at least one specific module, faculty member's research, or departmental feature
Connect it to a personal interest or gap in your current knowledge
Show you understand what makes this department's approach distinctive
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was drawn in partly by the breadth of the course at this level — the way it runs language and literary study alongside ancient history and archaeology rather than siloing them. I read about the papyrology work being done here, which is directly relevant to my interest in how texts survive and change in transmission. And the option to study ancient philosophy as a core strand rather than an elective is exactly the kind of integration I've been looking for. This feels like the department where those interests don't pull against each other.

commonMistakes: Relying on prestige or league-table rankings
Describing the course in terms that would fit any Classics department
Confusing this department's focus with another university's offering
followUps: What would you contribute to the academic life of this department?

question: Tell us about your experience reading Latin or Greek. What have you read that you found particularly rewarding?
lookingFor: Real engagement with the original language — the ability to say something substantive about a specific text, not just name it.

bullets: Name a specific text and say something about its content or style, not just its title
Reflect on what reading in the original language adds over translation
Be honest about the level reached without underselling genuine effort
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been studying Latin for four years and read extended passages of Caesar and Cicero at school. What I found most rewarding was a section of Cicero's Pro Caelio — the way he builds a persona for Clodia and then tears it down is so controlled, and you only see the full mechanism when you're watching the Latin closely. Translation smooths it over. I've also started Greek this year and I'm working through Athenaze; I can read simple prose passages but I know I need supervised teaching to get anywhere near the texts I want to read.

commonMistakes: Naming texts without saying anything specific about them
Overstating reading ability only to be caught out in the interview
Not acknowledging the difference between reading in translation and in the original
followUps: What did reading that text in the original teach you that a translation couldn't?

subtopic: A classical text that challenged you
question: Which classical text have you found most challenging to engage with, and what made it difficult?
lookingFor: Intellectual honesty about genuine difficulty, plus the ability to articulate where the challenge lies — linguistic, cultural, moral, or interpretive.

bullets: Name a specific text and be precise about the nature of the difficulty
Show that you pushed through rather than setting it aside
Demonstrate that the difficulty was intellectually productive
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Thucydides in translation was harder than I expected. I thought it would read like a narrative history, and instead I found myself in these long, dense speeches where the arguments coil back on themselves. It took me a while to understand what he was doing: that the speeches are reconstructed arguments, not transcripts, and that the difficulty is part of the point — he wants you to work to hold the positions in your head. Once I understood that, the Melian Dialogue stopped feeling impenetrable and became the most disturbing thing I'd read. The difficulty was the lesson.

commonMistakes: Naming a text that's considered difficult but failing to explain why it is for you
Treating difficulty as purely linguistic rather than conceptual
Giving up on the text without extracting anything from the struggle
followUps: Would you approach it differently now, and how?

question: Do you see yourself as more drawn to the literary side of Classics or the historical side, and why?
lookingFor: A thoughtful position that shows awareness of how the two are intertwined, not a simple binary preference.

bullets: State a genuine lean without pretending there's no preference
Demonstrate understanding of how history and literature inform each other in Classics
Give a concrete example of when one side illuminated the other
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My instinct is literary — I gravitated toward Classics through poetry and rhetoric rather than events and dates. But I've found I can't separate them cleanly. Reading the Aeneid without knowing what Augustan settlement meant politically leaves half the poem invisible. And studying the Gracchi without Plutarch's shaping hand gives you only bones. If I had to say: I approach from the literary side, but I've learned that the history is what gives the texts their weight. I think studying both properly is what makes a Classicist rather than just a reader.

commonMistakes: Refusing to take any position to seem balanced
Treating history and literature as entirely separate disciplines within Classics
No concrete examples to support the stated preference
followUps: Name a moment where the historical context completely changed how you read a text.

question: What have you read about the ancient world outside your school syllabus that has genuinely shaped how you think about Classics?
lookingFor: Evidence of independent intellectual curiosity — reading that goes beyond set texts, whether primary, secondary, or archaeological.

bullets: Name a specific book or article with an author, not just a vague area of interest
Explain what it opened up or changed in your thinking
Show it connects to the broader course, not just a hobby interest
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read Mary Beard's SPQR last year and it reframed how I thought about Roman identity. Her argument that being Roman was always more about behaviour and aspiration than blood or place challenged the way I'd been picturing the empire as ethnically bounded. Then I went looking for the primary sources she was drawing on, particularly Cicero's speeches on citizenship, and I started to see Roman history as a set of live arguments rather than settled facts. That shift — from history as narrative to history as debate — is what convinced me Classics was the right course.

commonMistakes: Citing only popular history books without engaging with their arguments
Mentioning a book but being unable to say what it actually argues
Reading only about Greece or only about Rome with no cross-pollination
followUps: Did you disagree with anything in that book? What?

question: Why does studying the ancient world still matter? How would you defend Classics to a sceptic?
lookingFor: A genuine, considered defence that avoids platitudes — the ability to argue for the field's intellectual value without being defensive.

bullets: Identify a specific way ancient thought or history illuminates something contemporary
Avoid the generic 'roots of Western civilisation' line unless you can do something substantive with it
Show you've thought about the field's limits and contested nature, not just its virtues
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd resist the 'foundation of Western civilisation' line because it's both too vague and too narrow — it writes out the Islamic transmission of Greek philosophy, for a start. My argument would be more precise: Classics gives us detailed, durable case studies in democracy's failure modes, in how propaganda shapes historiography, in the ethics of empire. Thucydides on Athenian overreach is genuinely useful for reading 21st-century foreign policy. And the philological training — learning to read with extreme care for what a text actually says versus what we want it to say — is a transferable intellectual discipline. The sceptic usually wants utility; I'd show them the Melian Dialogue and ask if it's outdated.

commonMistakes: Defaulting to 'Classics is the foundation of everything' without unpacking it
Being unable to name anything specific that Classics illuminates
Getting defensive rather than making a positive argument
followUps: Is there a part of the classical legacy you think has been overvalued or misused?

question: Which Greek tragedy have you read or seen that has stayed with you, and what do you think it is actually about?
lookingFor: A real interpretive position on a specific play — not a plot summary but a claim about its meaning.

bullets: Name a specific play and commit to an interpretation
Go beyond 'it's about fate' to something more precise
Engage with why the play is still performed and read — what makes it live
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Sophocles' Antigone has stayed with me most. The obvious reading is the clash between divine and human law, but what interests me more is that both Antigone and Creon are right, and the tragedy is that the play won't let either survive their rightness. Antigone cannot bend; Creon cannot admit error until it's too late. What it's about, I think, is the cost of consistency — of holding a principle so firmly that you lose the person in front of you. That feels more contemporary to me than the religious law question. I've seen one production and read the Fagles translation carefully alongside the Greek, and the question it leaves is: who would I have been?

commonMistakes: Summarising the plot instead of interpreting the play
Claiming a play is 'about fate' with nothing more specific
Picking the most obvious interpretation without showing any original thought
followUps: Does knowing the original Athenian context change how you read the play?

question: If you had to choose between the Iliad and the Odyssey, which would you say is the greater poem, and why?
lookingFor: A genuine aesthetic and intellectual judgement with reasoning — shows engagement with both poems, not just familiarity with one.

bullets: Make an actual choice rather than refusing to decide
Offer specific textual reasons, not just 'I prefer action over adventure'
Show awareness of what you might be undervaluing in the other poem
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The Iliad, and I say that even knowing the Odyssey is more structurally intricate. What gives the Iliad its power is its concentration: everything converges on the grief of two men — Achilles over Patroclus, and Priam over Hector — and the poem holds that grief with extraordinary care. Book 24, where Priam goes to Achilles to beg for his son's body, is the most emotionally precise thing I've read in any literature. The Odyssey is brilliant on disguise, desire, homecoming, but it diffuses its energy across years and islands. The Iliad burns. I'd grant the Odyssey the cleverer plot; the Iliad has the heavier heart.

commonMistakes: Refusing to choose in order to seem fair-minded
Making the choice without any textual evidence
Being unable to say anything generous about the poem you didn't choose
followUps: Which character from either poem would you most want to interrogate, and what would you ask?

question: How reliable do you think ancient historians like Livy or Tacitus are as sources? How do we read them critically?
lookingFor: Methodological awareness — understanding that ancient historians are interpreters, not just recorders, and that their biases are themselves evidence.

bullets: Acknowledge that ancient historians had rhetorical and political purposes alongside factual ones
Explain at least one method for reading them critically — comparison, archaeology, internal contradiction
Avoid dismissing them entirely: they are primary evidence even when unreliable as fact
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Livy and Tacitus are both valuable and treacherous in different ways. Livy writing in the early Augustan period is partly constructing Roman moral identity; his early books are legend shaped into exemplary narrative. That doesn't make him useless — it makes him primary evidence for how Romans wanted to understand their own past. Tacitus is closer to events but has his own politics: his hostility to the Principate shapes what he sees. The critical approach is to read them against each other, against documentary evidence like inscriptions, and to track where they contradict themselves. The bias is data. The question isn't 'is this true?' but 'why is this being said this way, and what does that tell us?'

commonMistakes: Either accepting ancient historians as straightforwardly factual or dismissing them as simply biased
No awareness of the methodological tools available to a historian of antiquity
Treating Livy and Tacitus as interchangeable when their contexts and purposes differ
followUps: Can you think of a case where two ancient sources flatly contradict each other? How would you adjudicate?

question: Have you read any ancient philosophy? What did you make of it?
lookingFor: Engagement with a specific philosophical argument or dialogue — evidence that philosophy is part of the applicant's Classics, not just literature and history.

bullets: Name a specific dialogue or philosopher and say something about an argument, not just a theme
Show that you engaged with the reasoning rather than just the famous positions
Reflect on what ancient philosophy offers that modern philosophy doesn't — or vice versa
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've read Plato's Apology and parts of the Republic. What struck me in the Apology was the argument Socrates makes for why he won't go into exile: that a life without philosophical inquiry is not worth living. What's interesting isn't just the claim but the structure — he builds it on a commitment to following argument wherever it leads, regardless of social cost. I found myself disagreeing with him about whether that's the only valuable life, but the Apology made me think carefully about what grounds that disagreement. The Republic's treatment of justice felt more difficult to accept, particularly the Noble Lie, but I want to engage with it more rigorously than I could on my own.

commonMistakes: Treating Plato and Aristotle as essentially the same thinker
Describing philosophical themes without engaging with the actual arguments
Pretending to have read texts in depth when your knowledge is shallow
followUps: Is there an ancient philosophical argument you found genuinely persuasive?

question: How does archaeology change or deepen what we can know about the ancient world that texts alone can't tell us?
lookingFor: Understanding that material evidence and textual evidence are different in kind, not just degree — and that each corrects and challenges the other.

bullets: Give a specific example where archaeology revealed something texts ignored or contradicted
Show that material evidence has its own interpretive problems — it doesn't just speak plainly
Demonstrate genuine interest in this side of Classics, not just tolerance of it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Texts tend to reflect the concerns of the literate elite: politics, rhetoric, theology. Archaeology reaches the everyday — the grain prices scratched on an ostrakon, the layout of a working-class insula in Pompeii, the graffiti on its walls. When you look at Pompeii's electoral notices alongside its street shrines to lares, you get a picture of civic religion that's much messier and more local than the literary sources suggest. But I've also learned that material evidence has its own interpretation problems — a cooking pot doesn't tell you whether it was used for ritual or daily meals. The two types of evidence are better in dialogue than in isolation.

commonMistakes: Treating archaeology as simply 'more evidence' rather than a different kind of evidence
No specific example — only general claims about what archaeology 'can do'
Ignoring the interpretive challenges of material evidence
followUps: Is there an archaeological discovery that has changed scholarly understanding of a text or period?

question: Why does it matter to read classical texts in the original language rather than a good modern translation?
lookingFor: A substantive argument for philological engagement — showing awareness of what translation necessarily loses and what the original preserves.

bullets: Give a specific example of something a translation cannot convey — metre, wordplay, ambiguity, register
Acknowledge that good translations are valuable rather than dismissing them
Connect the argument to your own experience reading even partial originals
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: A translation is always an interpretation, and it resolves ambiguities that the original leaves open. The Greek word 'dike' means justice, custom, and trial all at once, and each English rendering forecloses the others. In the Oresteia, that ambiguity is the whole point — Aeschylus wants the word to be doing multiple things simultaneously. A translator has to choose, and in choosing, shapes what the play is about. I've also noticed even at my level that Latin poetry is made of sound as much as meaning: Virgil's spondees in the underworld passages don't translate. You can note them in an introduction, but you can't hear them in English.

commonMistakes: Dismissing translations as simply inadequate without acknowledging their genuine value
Making the argument in the abstract with no concrete example
Claiming translations are useless when most Classics study at any level involves reading them
followUps: Which translation of a classical text do you think is the best available, and why?

question: How do you think about Athens versus Sparta — is the contrast that gets taught at school accurate, or does it oversimplify?
lookingFor: Critical awareness of the sources — particularly that our picture of Sparta comes largely from Athenian and later Roman writers — without losing the ability to make meaningful distinctions.

bullets: Acknowledge that the contrast is partly an Athenian construction ('laconophilia' in philosophy and later Roman nostalgia)
Show genuine knowledge of at least one way the received view is complicated
Still be able to say what the comparison is genuinely useful for
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The contrast is useful but it's built almost entirely on non-Spartan sources, which is the first problem. The idealised Sparta of Plato and Plutarch was partly a rhetorical tool — a way for Athenian critics of democracy to invoke a better-organised alternative. The real Sparta probably had more internal political debate than the sources allow. And Athenian democracy was considerably more exclusionary than its self-image suggests: no women, no slaves, no metics. That said, the comparison is still live as a question about what civic education and military culture do to a society, and I think studying both alongside each other honestly — not as archetypes — is where the interesting questions are.

commonMistakes: Accepting the school-level contrast uncritically
Overcorrecting to 'everything you know is wrong' without substance
Not knowing that most Spartan 'evidence' comes from hostile or nostalgic outside sources
followUps: If you could excavate any Spartan site to test one claim about Sparta, which would it be?

question: Was the fall of the Roman Republic inevitable? How do you think about the transition to the Principate?
lookingFor: Historical reasoning with agency — an ability to see contingency as well as structural causes without reducing the question to a simple verdict.

bullets: Resist 'inevitable' as a total explanation — name at least one contingent factor
Show awareness of the structural pressures: army loyalty, wealth inequality, provincial governance
Engage with the historiographical question: how did Romans themselves understand what happened?
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I resist 'inevitable' because it forecloses the contingency. The structural pressures were real — the army's loyalty shifting to generals rather than senate, the social tensions the Gracchi were trying to resolve, the impossibility of governing a Mediterranean empire through republican institutions designed for a city-state. But Caesar's assassination could have played out differently; Augustus was unusually gifted at wrapping autocracy in republican language. What interests me is the ideological work required: Augustus kept the forms — consuls, senate, tribunes — while changing the substance. The Romans who lived through it debated whether anything had changed, and that debate shapes Tacitus entirely. The fall wasn't inevitable; the Republic's difficulty surviving empire probably was.

commonMistakes: Treating Augustus as simply a dictator who ended the Republic overnight
Attributing the fall purely to individual leaders without structural causes
No engagement with how contemporaries or near-contemporaries understood the transition
followUps: Which ancient writer do you think has the most penetrating analysis of why the Republic fell?

question: How do you approach texts from a world in which women, enslaved people, and outsiders are largely written out of the record?
lookingFor: Methodological sophistication — awareness of the representational limits of the classical corpus without abandoning it as evidence.

bullets: Acknowledge whose voices dominate the literary record and why
Identify at least one method for recovering marginalised perspectives — papyri, archaeology, reading against the grain
Show this is an intellectually generative problem, not just a politically correct box to tick
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The corpus we have is almost entirely male, freeborn, and citizen in perspective, which is a representational problem and also an intellectual one — we're working with a partial archive. But the response isn't to give up: it's to read more carefully. Epigraphy gives us women's dedications and freedmen's epitaphs. Egyptian papyri preserve private letters, including from women who are invisible in literary sources. And reading against the grain of a text like the Lysistrata, or tracking the moments when authors like Ovid speak for enslaved characters in a way that reveals the author's anxieties rather than the slave's — that's evidence too. The absence is shaped, and a shaped absence is recoverable. It's one of the things I find most methodologically interesting about the field.

commonMistakes: Treating the partial archive as simply a 'gap to fill in' with modern assumptions
Not knowing that alternative evidence (papyri, epigraphy, archaeology) exists
Dismissing literary sources entirely because of their perspective
followUps: Can you name a text where a marginalised perspective breaks through, even partially?

question: Can you give an example of a classical text or idea that has been misused or distorted in its later reception? Why does that matter for how we study Classics?
lookingFor: Critical awareness that reception shapes what we think the ancients said, and that clean philology matters as a corrective.

bullets: Name a specific case of misappropriation — fascist aesthetics of Rome, social Darwinism and Thucydides, etc.
Explain what was distorted and why the original text resists that reading
Connect this to the value of accurate philological and historical study
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The most obvious case is the appropriation of Roman imagery — the eagle, the fasces, the legions — by European fascism in the twentieth century. But the distortion runs deeper than aesthetics: there's a tradition of reading Thucydides' 'the strong do what they can and the weak suffer what they must' as a realist endorsement of power politics, when the Melian Dialogue actually dramatises the Athenian position as a tragedy leading directly to Syracuse. Thucydides doesn't endorse the Athenians; he shows us where their logic leads. The misreading matters because it's been used in policy debates. Which is exactly why reading the text carefully, in context, rather than through quotation, is not just an academic exercise.

commonMistakes: Identifying a case without being able to say what the original text actually argues
Treating reception as only a modern political problem rather than an ancient one too
No connection between the example and why accurate scholarship matters
followUps: Is there a reception of a classical text you think has been genuinely illuminating rather than distorting?

question: What do you think Greek myths were actually for? How should we read them?
lookingFor: An understanding that myths are not simply pre-scientific explanations but cultural and social texts — and some awareness of scholarly approaches.

bullets: Move beyond 'myths explain natural phenomena' to a richer account
Show awareness of at least one scholarly framework — structuralism, ritual function, political legitimation
Ground the argument in a specific myth rather than talking only in the abstract
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The simple 'myths explain thunder' account doesn't hold up: the Greeks had multiple, contradictory cosmological myths and clearly weren't bothered by the contradiction. What myths do more reliably is work out social anxieties. The Oedipus story isn't about fate in some abstract sense — it's working on questions about kinship, identity, pollution, what happens when the boundaries between categories collapse. Lévi-Strauss's structural approach interested me, the idea that myths mediate oppositions the culture can't resolve, but I'm wary of it becoming too mechanical. What I think is most honest is: myths were doing multiple things at once — religious, civic, entertaining, political — and the same story gets reused in different contexts for different purposes. Pindar's Pelops is not Sophocles' Pelops.

commonMistakes: Treating Greek myths as a single unified system rather than a contested and evolving body of stories
No awareness that the same myth is told differently by different authors for different purposes
Reducing myths to either pure allegory or pure entertainment
followUps: Which myth do you think is doing the most interesting cultural work, and in which telling?

question: What do you make of Cicero — is he a genuine thinker, or primarily a political operator who happened to write well?
lookingFor: A considered position on the relationship between rhetoric, philosophy, and political action in the ancient world — not a character judgement but an intellectual one.

bullets: Resist the binary: show that Cicero's political situation and his philosophy are intertwined
Engage with at least one specific work or argument
Show understanding that for Cicero, rhetoric and philosophy were not in opposition
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The dichotomy feels anachronistic to me. Cicero would have rejected the idea that a thinker could operate outside political life — his philosophical writings, De Re Publica in particular, are genuinely trying to work out what just government looks like, and they're doing it in the context of a Republic he watched fracture. The Tusculan Disputations were written when he'd lost political power, and they read like someone thinking hard about Stoic consolation because he needed it, not as exercises. Is he self-serving? Repeatedly, yes. But the Pro Caelio is also a masterpiece of constructed character, and the Philippics show real moral courage at real personal risk. I think he's both things, and that's what makes him interesting rather than simply admirable or contemptible.

commonMistakes: Dismissing Cicero as a mere politician without engaging with his philosophical work
Treating him as a pure philosopher with no political calculation
No specific engagement with any text
followUps: Which of Cicero's philosophical works do you think holds up best as philosophy, rather than rhetoric?

question: What does studying an inflected language like Latin or Ancient Greek teach you about how language and meaning work?
lookingFor: Genuine linguistic reflection — awareness that Latin's case system and lack of fixed word order forces different choices than English, and what that reveals.

bullets: Explain what inflection means and what it allows that English's word-order grammar can't easily do
Give a specific example of a Latin or Greek construction that has no clean English equivalent
Reflect on what this tells you about the relationship between grammar and thought
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Latin's case system means word order can be free in ways English's can't be — the subject and object are marked by ending, not position. That frees the poet to put the emphatic word wherever emphasis is needed, which is why translating Latin poetry so often feels like a loss. Hyperbaton, the splitting of a noun from its adjective across several words, creates a kind of tension the reader has to hold and then resolve. More broadly, working with an inflected language made me notice how much English grammar I was just absorbing rather than understanding. Aspect in Greek — the difference between aorist and imperfect — is more granular than anything in English, and getting it right forces you to think carefully about what kind of action you mean. The language teaches you to be precise.

commonMistakes: Describing grammar rules without reflecting on what they reveal about thought or style
No specific linguistic example — only generalities
Treating Latin as just 'harder English' rather than a structurally different language
followUps: Is there a Latin or Greek grammatical feature you wish English had?

question: Which figure from the ancient world do you find most interesting, and what makes them worth studying?
lookingFor: Genuine engagement with a historical or literary figure — intellectual curiosity rather than hero-worship, with awareness of evidence problems.

bullets: Choose someone beyond the most obvious names unless you can say something genuinely fresh
Engage with why they are historically or intellectually significant, not just personally appealing
Show awareness of the evidence we have and what it can't tell us
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I keep coming back to Tacitus's Agricola as a figure: Gnaeus Julius Agricola, the general in Britain, partly because the source problem is so interesting. Everything we know comes from his son-in-law, Tacitus, who is also using him as a vehicle for criticising Domitian. So you have a real person filtered through a political eulogy filtered through some of the best Latin prose ever written. Is Agricola a good general? Probably. Is he Tacitus's ideal Roman? Certainly. You can't separate the two. That methodological puzzle feels very Classics to me: the figure is worth studying precisely because the evidence is so interested.

commonMistakes: Choosing Julius Caesar or Augustus and saying nothing that isn't common knowledge
Treating ancient biography as straightforward biographical fact
Admiring the figure without any critical distance
followUps: What would change your view of them if you discovered it was true?

question: Do you read the Aeneid as Augustan propaganda, or is that too simple? What is Virgil actually doing?
lookingFor: Critical engagement with the poem's political ambiguity — the 'Harvard School' reading of the Aeneid as subversive versus the optimistic reading, and whether the binary holds.

bullets: Acknowledge the Augustan framing without reducing the poem to propaganda
Identify at least one moment that complicates a simple celebratory reading — Turnus's death, Dido, the two gates of sleep
Offer a position on what Virgil is actually doing, however tentative
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The poem clearly serves Augustan purposes at the level of plot — Rome's founding legitimated, Aeneas's line traced to Augustus — but what Virgil does with that frame is less comfortable than a propaganda reading allows. Dido is too fully realised: the reader mourns her, and Aeneas's abandonment of her, even at Jupiter's command, feels like a cost the poem asks us to register. Turnus's death in the final lines — Aeneas killing him in rage, in passion rather than pietas — leaves the poem on a note that troubles the triumph. The two gates of sleep in Book 6 send the Trojan shades out through the ivory gate, the gate of false dreams. Whether that's a deliberate irony Virgil inserted for those who could see it, or a puzzle with no clean solution, I don't know. But it suggests the poem is more ambivalent about empire's costs than a panegyric would be.

commonMistakes: Treating the poem as either pure propaganda or purely subversive with no engagement with the text
Being unable to name a specific passage that complicates the simple reading
Not knowing that the 'two traditions' of Aeneid scholarship exist as a live debate
followUps: Does it matter whether Virgil intended the ambiguity, or only that it's there?

question: Classics draws on literary criticism, history, archaeology, linguistics, and philosophy. Which of those lenses do you find most productive, and are there any you're sceptical of?
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual positioning — a sense of which methodological approaches the applicant has actually encountered and what they think about them.

bullets: Name specific approaches and say something substantive about at least one
Show that you have a genuine preference grounded in intellectual reasons, not just habit
Be willing to express scepticism about an approach rather than treating all methods as equally valid
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I find the historical and philological lenses most productive together — meaning emerges from the specific context in which a text was produced and the precise language it uses. I'm more cautious about structuralist approaches: Lévi-Strauss is illuminating up to a point, but there's a risk of making the ancient world confirm the model rather than the other way around. And psychoanalytic readings of classical texts can feel anachronistic — I'm sceptical of projecting Freudian categories onto Oedipus when the play predates the theory by two and a half millennia. That said, I don't want to be parochial about it: Foucault's work on ancient sexuality opened up questions about the body and power that purely literary approaches missed. I want to know all the lenses and use the ones that earn their keep on a given text.

commonMistakes: Listing methods without saying anything about what each one does or how
Refusing to express any scepticism out of diplomatic caution
No evidence of actually having encountered secondary scholarship using any of these methods
followUps: Can you name a specific scholar whose methodological approach you've found useful or provocative?

subtopic: What you hope to take from a Classics degree
question: What do you hope to be able to do, think, or understand at the end of a Classics degree that you can't do now?
lookingFor: Intellectual ambition grounded in the specific substance of the subject — not just generic skills but the particular gains a Classics education offers.

bullets: Name specific competencies or understandings, not just 'I'll be more educated'
Show that the goals are shaped by what Classics specifically offers, not transferable to any humanities degree
Demonstrate awareness of where you currently are and where the gap lies
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: At the most concrete level, I want to be able to read Thucydides in Greek and Tacitus in Latin without an interlinear crib — to have the language fluently enough that I'm responding to the text, not to a translation of it. Beyond that, I want to be able to make a real historical argument from primary sources: to know how to use epigraphy, how to calibrate a literary source, how to hold three different types of evidence in tension. And I want to have genuinely wrestled with the philosophical tradition from Plato to the Stoics, not just summarised it. I think those three things — language, historical method, and philosophical engagement — are what distinguishes someone who has done Classics from someone who has read about it.

commonMistakes: Generic answers about 'critical thinking' and 'communication skills'
Goals that could come from any humanities degree
Not connecting the goals to the specific content of Classics
followUps: Which of those goals do you think will be hardest to achieve, and why?
